items.passage: Every living thing is made of small units called cells. Cells have a thin outer skin that lets some things in and keeps other things out. Inside each cell, tiny parts work together to make energy and build new pieces. Many cells also send signals to nearby cells so they can grow and act as a team.

National parks protect land where rare plants and animals can live in safety. Park staff watch the forest and the rivers and keep visitors on marked paths. They also count the birds each spring to learn how many young ones are born. Without this careful work, many wild places would slowly disappear under roads and farms.

Why do some people keep working at a goal when others quickly give up? Studies show that small daily rewards help most people stay on track for a long time. People also do better when they share their plan with a friend who checks in often. However, very large rewards can make the task feel like work and lower interest fast.

Long ago, traders carried silk, spices, and books between China and the cities of central Asia. Along the way, they shared songs and stories with the people they met at each stop. New ideas about music and food spread quickly from one town to the next. Many of these old trade paths are still marked on maps today.

Fossils are old prints or hard parts of plants and animals saved in rock. They form when a body is buried in mud before it can rot away. Over many years, slow changes turn the soft mud into hard stone around it. Scientists then study these old shapes to learn how life on earth has changed.

Robots have helped people learn about planets that are too far away to visit. Small machines on wheels move across the dry red ground of Mars and send back clear photos. They also test the soil to find out if water was once there. These quiet helpers have taught us more about space than any book alone could.
